patient.communicationStyle: Cooperative but frightened. Wants reassurance. May minimize symptoms initially due to denial.
patient.emotionalState: Anxious, scared, sense of impending doom
patient.languageNotes: English fluent. May use lay terms like 'sugar' for diabetes, 'pressure' for hypertension.

chiefComplaint.severity: 8-10/10, worst chest discomfort of my life
chiefComplaint.timing: Started at rest, while watching TV / in bed
aggravating: not worse with breathing or movement

absent: pleuritic component (not worse with deep breath)

socialHistory.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, 1-2 per week
socialHistory.occupation: Teacher, mostly desk work, moderate stress

familyHistory: Mother: Heart attack at age 58, deceased

physicalExam.general: Alert, anxious-appearing, diaphoretic, in moderate distress, clutching chest
physicalExam.heent: Pupils equal, reactive. Mucous membranes moist.
physicalExam.neck: Supple, no JVD, no carotid bruits
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs/rubs/gallops. PMI non-displaced.
physicalExam.pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi
physicalExam.abdominal: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds
physicalExam.extremities: No edema, 2+ pulses bilaterally, warm and well-perfused
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert and oriented x4, no focal deficits

findings: Reciprocal ST depressions in III, aVF

imaging.findings: Cardiac silhouette upper limits of normal. Mild pulmonary vascular congestion. No focal consolidation or pneumothorax.
imaging.impression: Early pulmonary vascular congestion, no acute cardiopulmonary process

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: HR decreases to 80s, BP normalizes to 130s/80s, SpO2 improves to 98% on 2L NC
onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Chest pressure resolving post-PCI, patient reports 2/10 discomfort
onCorrectTreatment.labsTrend: Troponin peaks at 12 hours then trends down over 24-48 hours
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: Patient taken emergently to cath lab. LAD 100% occlusion found and stented. Good flow restored. Patient comfortable post-procedure.

onDelayedTreatment.vitalsTrend: HR increases to 120s, BP drops to 100s/60s, SpO2 drops to 90%
onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Patient becoming more diaphoretic and hypotensive. Repeat ECG shows expanding ST elevations. Becoming confused.

onNoTreatment.narrative: Patient found unresponsive. Code blue called. VF arrest on monitor.

keyLearningPoints: Classic STEMI presentation: substernal pressure + radiation + diaphoresis + nausea
Women may present with atypical symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, nausea)
Time is muscle - door-to-balloon <90 minutes is the goal
Rising troponin with ischemic symptoms = acute coronary syndrome
Reciprocal ECG changes increase specificity for STEMI
mustNotMiss: Aortic dissection (check for tearing pain, back radiation, pulse deficits)
Pulmonary embolism (check for pleuritic pain, leg symptoms, risk factors)
Tension pneumothorax (check for unilateral breath sounds, trauma)
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on GERD in patient with cardiac risk factors
Not getting ECG within 10 minutes of arrival
Missing posterior STEMI (get posterior leads if high suspicion)
Delaying cath lab activation for confirmatory troponin

differentialWithProbabilities.reasoning: Classic presentation with risk factors, diaphoresis, and radiation
Similar presentation, would need ECG to differentiate
Must rule out given severity, but less likely without tearing quality or back pain
Less likely without pleuritic component or DVT symptoms
Would expect positional component and possibly friction rub

keyQuestions: Can you describe the pain - is it pressure, sharp, or tearing?
Does it radiate anywhere - arm, jaw, back?
Are you sweating more than usual?
Is it worse when you breathe deeply or change position?
Any leg swelling or recent travel?
expertApproach.reasoning: This 52-year-old female with multiple cardiac risk factors (DM, HTN, HLD, family history) presents with substernal pressure radiating to the left arm and jaw, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. This is a classic STEMI presentation. The absence of pleuritic or positional components makes pericarditis and PE less likely. The gradual onset and pressure quality argue against dissection. ECG and emergent cardiology consultation are the immediate priorities.

teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutFamilyHistory: Mother's death from MI at 58 is highly relevant - this is a major risk factor and also explains patient's anxiety
teachingHooks.ifMissesRadiation: Patient might rub left shoulder unconsciously or mention 'my arm feels funny'
teachingHooks.ifDoesntAskAboutDiaphoresis: Observant student would note patient is visibly sweating
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutMedCompliance: Metformin compliance issue is relevant for management but not diagnosis
teachingHooks.ifConsidersDissection: Good safety thinking - absence of tearing, maximal-at-onset, and back pain makes it less likely

patient.communicationStyle: Generally healthy person who is surprised to be in the ED. Concerned about returning to normal activities.

chiefComplaint.duration: Comes and goes, worse with movement/breathing
chiefComplaint.severity: 7/10 at worst, 3/10 when still and leaning forward
chiefComplaint.timing: Worse in the morning, when lying down

present: viral illness 1 week ago (sore throat, runny nose, mild fever)

physicalExam.general: Young adult, appears mildly uncomfortable, sitting forward
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, THREE-COMPONENT FRICTION RUB heard at left sternal border (scratchy, squeaky sound), no murmurs

findings: Diffuse ST elevations (concave up) in multiple leads

keyLearningPoints: Pericarditis: sharp/pleuritic pain, better leaning forward, friction rub
ECG shows diffuse ST elevation (concave up) and PR depression
Recent viral illness is common precipitant
Friction rub is pathognomonic but may come and go
Most cases are idiopathic/viral and self-limited
mustNotMiss: STEMI (check for risk factors, pressure quality, radiation)
Aortic dissection (check for tearing, back pain, pulse deficits)
Cardiac tamponade (check for hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds)
